title: Refresh token rotation — timeline reference
description: Reference data illustrating the refresh rotation chain + reuse-detection breach signal. Each row represents a request against POST /auth/refresh; the state transitions of auth_refresh_tokens rows are shown side-by-side with the events fired.

state_change: INSERT auth_refresh_tokens (id=atr_01, family_id=fam_A, token_hash=hash(A), consumed_at=NULL, tokenable=Alice.User)
UPDATE auth_refresh_tokens SET consumed_at = T+15min WHERE id = atr_01
INSERT auth_refresh_tokens (id=atr_02, family_id=fam_A, token_hash=hash(B), consumed_at=NULL, tokenable=Alice.User)
UPDATE personal_access_tokens SET revoked_at = T+15min WHERE id = 42
UPDATE auth_refresh_tokens SET consumed_at = T+30min WHERE id = atr_02
UPDATE personal_access_tokens SET revoked_at = T+30min WHERE id = 43

reuse_detection_timeline.action: Attacker (who stole atr_01 from Alice's device) POST /auth/refresh (presents atr_01)
state_change: UPDATE auth_refresh_tokens SET consumed_at = T+15min WHERE id = atr_01
reuse_detection_timeline.note: Attacker now holds atr_02 + PAT 99. Alice STILL holds her copy of atr_01 (thinks it's valid).

state_change: SELECT auth_refresh_tokens WHERE token_hash = hash(atr_01) → row found; consumed_at IS NOT NULL
events_fired: UPDATE auth_refresh_tokens SET revoked_at = T+25min WHERE family_id = fam_A
Enumerate every active PAT for Alice.User → UPDATE personal_access_tokens SET revoked_at = T+25min WHERE id IN (99)

client_response.note: DELIBERATELY opaque — not REFRESH_TOKEN_REUSE_DETECTED so an attacker holding a replayed token can't confirm from the response body that they've been detected.

reuse_detection_timeline.next_step_for_alice: Alice's client falls back to login screen. Alice receives 'Unusual activity' email + reviews /auth/sessions to see the attacker's session (revoked) + investigates.

invariants: family_id is UNIQUE PER LOGIN. Every rotation from that login carries the same family_id — the chain is queryable.
consumed_at is SET ATOMICALLY inside the same transaction as INSERT-new-row. No client-observable window between old-consumed and new-issued.

Reuse detection triggers on the second presentation of a consumed token, regardless of which client presents it (attacker OR legitimate). Symmetric — the attacker's presentation of atr_02 (after atr_01 was consumed) also fires the same signal.

The response to a reuse-detection event is DELIBERATELY the same as a plain invalid-token response. This prevents attackers from confirming they've been detected via response-body / response-status timing.
Every session revocation cascades — revoking atr_02 also revokes PAT 99 (attacker's). Revoking Alice's active PAT means Alice reloads to a logged-out state and re-authenticates.
The security notification to Alice fires ONLY if she has the identity.suspicious_login category on (default opt-in). Alice can opt out but the audit-log entry always fires.
